title: Subjective logic as a complementary tool to meta-analysis to explicitly address second-order uncertainty in research findings: A case from infant studies
abstract: Any experiment brings about results and conclusions that seemingly have a component of uncertainty. Many factors influence that degree of that uncertainty, yet they can be overlooked when drawing conclusions from a body of evidence. Here, we discuss how subjective logic and the concept of a subjective opinion can provide empirical scientists with the chance to reason about this uncertainty into the answer that researchers seek to provide to their research questions. We illustrate this approach by focusing on a body of evidence already meta-analysed, as from this starting point we can show how each finding can be encoded as a subjective opinion, and how findings can be aggregated to produce an answer that explicitly accounts for the chosen sources of uncertainty.
